coredata.dc:title: N-doped antioxidant carbon dots as a bimodal probe for nitrite detection in commercial meat products
coredata.dc:creator: Alkaltham, Mohammed S.
Althawab, Suleiman A.
Alsulami, Tawfiq
Alzahrani, Abdulhakeem
coredata.dc:description: Accurate, sensitive, and specific nitrite ion (NO2 −) detection is critical for maintaining the quality of food and water, given its ubiquitous presence in the environment. This investigation introduces the development of nitrogen-infused carbon dots, which serve dual functions as antioxidants and NO2 −sensors in food products. These carbon dots (CDs) facilitates bimodal NO2 − detection, combining precise fluorimetric detection and straightforward colorimetric analysis. Upon introduction into the solution, NO2 − interacts with the amino groups on the surface of the CDs, resulting in the formation of a diazotized compound and causing a color change in the solution. Notably, the detection limits for NO2 − are determined to be 0.19 μM and 0.144 μM, respectively. In addition, the bimode probe demonstrates exceptional consistency and recovery in determining NO2 − concentrations in commercial samples. The radical scavenging activity of the CDs is assessed using the 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and 2,2′-azinobis-(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS) tests, revealing robust antioxidant activity (~78 % DPPH) and (>80 % for ABTS). Furthermore, cell viability is evaluated through the metabolic activity of fibroblast (NIH 3 T3) cells. Due to their antioxidant properties and low toxicity to human cell lines, these CDs represent a versatile and useful tool for biomedical research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Antioxidant activity
Biocompatibility
Bimodal detection
Nitrite
Colorimetry
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantification of nitrite in commercial products
Plausible mechanistic pathway of nitrite sensing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to extend their sincere appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, for funding this work through the project number (RSPD2024R917).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: schematic illustration of SLCDs via one-step thermal pyrolysis process.
(a) TEM image of the SLCDs, (b) Size distribution of the SLCDs obtained from TEM (c), UV–vis spectrum of the SLCDs (d), excitation dependent PL spectra of SLCDs, and (e) FTIR plot of the SLCDs.
(a) XPS survey scan of SLCDs. High resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s.
The antioxidant efficiency of SLCDs at various concentrations includes the following methods: (a) DPPH assay, (b) ABTS assay, and (c) radical scavenging mechanisms of SLCDs.

pH value of the (SLCDs + NO2 −) system showing significant impact on both the absorbance intensity ratio (A386/A533) and FL quenching efficiency ((F0 F)/F0). Effects of the concentration of SLCDs on A386/A533 and (F0 F)/F0 are shown in (c) and (d), respectively. Effects of the incubation temperature on A386/A533 and (F0 F)/F0 can be observed in (e) and (f), respectively. The concentration of SLCDs and NO2 − in the final solution is 15 mg/mL and 33 μM, respectively, except for Fig. 3 (c) and (d). Error bars are determined based on three replicate measurements.

Impact of varying reaction times between SLCDs and NO2 − on (a) the ratio of absorbance intensity (A386/A533) and (b) FL quenching efficiency ((F0 F)/F0). The concentration of SLCDs and NO2 − in the solution is 15 mg/mL and 33 μM, respectively.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and (c) FL emission spectra of the SLCDs dispersion in the presence of different NO2 − concentrations. Calibration plot of (b) absorbance intensity ratio (A386/A533) and (d) FL quenching efficiency ((F0 F)/F0) vs the NO2 − concentration. In all the above detection systems, the concentration of SLCDs is 15 mg/mL.

(a) Absorption signal intensity ratio (A386/A533) and (b) FL quenching efficacy ((F0 F)/F0) of the SLCDs system with various analytes of interest. The concentration of NO2 − is kept at 20 μM, with a concentration of 150 μM of interfering materials. The SLCDs concentration in all the detection methods is 15 mg/mL.

(a) UV–vis spectrum of nitrite and FL excitation and emission of SLCDs, (b) UV–vis spectrum of SLCDs, SLCDs + NO2 −, and NO2 −, FTIR spectra of NO2 −, SLCDs, and SLCDs + NO2 −, and (d) FL decay curve of SLCDs (450 nm) with and without NO2 −.
Cell viability assays of fibroblast (NIH 3 T3) cells at different concentrations of SLCDs.
Comparison of various nanomaterials-based sensing probes for the nitrite detection.

head.ce:title: N-doped antioxidant carbon dots as a bimodal probe for nitrite detection in commercial meat products

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science & Nutrition, College of Food and Agricultural Sciences, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Agricultural Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science & Nutrition, College of Food and Agricultural Sciences, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate, sensitive, and specific nitrite ion (NO2 −) detection is critical for maintaining the quality of food and water, given its ubiquitous presence in the environment. This investigation introduces the development of nitrogen-infused carbon dots, which serve dual functions as antioxidants and NO2 −sensors in food products. These carbon dots (CDs) facilitates bimodal NO2 − detection, combining precise fluorimetric detection and straightforward colorimetric analysis. Upon introduction into the solution, NO2 − interacts with the amino groups on the surface of the CDs, resulting in the formation of a diazotized compound and causing a color change in the solution. Notably, the detection limits for NO2 − are determined to be 0.19 μM and 0.144 μM, respectively. In addition, the bimode probe demonstrates exceptional consistency and recovery in determining NO2 − concentrations in commercial samples. The radical scavenging activity of the CDs is assessed using the 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and 2,2′-azinobis-(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS) tests, revealing robust antioxidant activity (~78 % DPPH) and (>80 % for ABTS). Furthermore, cell viability is evaluated through the metabolic activity of fibroblast (NIH 3 T3) cells. Due to their antioxidant properties and low toxicity to human cell lines, these CDs represent a versatile and useful tool for biomedical research.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite ions (NO2 −) are widely used as chemical fertilizers in agriculture and as preservatives in the food industry. However, even trace amounts can cause considerable environmental pollution, negatively affecting human well-being [1]. As a result, monitoring NO2 − ions is a critical role for assessing inflammation, food safety, and water quality. NO2 − ions play a critical role in the formation of highly carcinogenic N-nitrosamines through their reaction with amides/amines [2]. These ions possess a diverse range of chemical properties and can form strong molecular bonds with proteins. For example, hemoglobin can lose its ability to transport oxygen in the blood due to irreversible oxidation caused by NO2 − ions [3]. Additionally, the reaction between nitrites and amines in food can result in the formation of carcinogenic N-nitrosamines, which have been linked to the development of stomach cancer and “Blue Baby Syndrome” [4]. The US Environmental Protection Agency has set a maximum contaminant level for NO2 − at 1 ppm (or 21.7 μM), while the World Health Organization recommends a similar guideline of 3 ppm (or 65 μM) [5]. Consequently, there has been a notable surge in developing novel methods to detect NO2 − ions in food, water, and the environment, with a strong emphasis on enhancing sensitivity. Various analytical methods have been proposed for the NO2 − detection, including spectrophotometry [6,7], chromatography [8], chemiluminescence [9], electrochemiluminescence [10], biosensing [11], and electrochemical approaches [12–14]. Thus, there is a pressing need to develop an eco-friendly, straightforward, sensitive, cost-effective, and accurate approach for detecting NO2 − in different surroundings. Such a method would be highly valuable and revolutionary for both environmental monitoring and clinical diagnostics. Despite these advancements, many current techniques remain limited by time-consuming procedures, expensive equipment, and low sensitivity, which reduce their practicality and increace costs. Among the other nanomaterials, fluorescent CDs stand out due to their desirable characteristics, including cost-effectiveness, excellent photostability, ease synthesis, low toxicity, and exceptional photoluminescence [15–17]. These numerous properties render CDs a highly promising material for developing novel nanosensors with remarkable stability and sensitivity [18,19]. Despite their widespread use as fluorescent indicators for metal ions, their application in detecting NO2 − ions remains relatively underexplored.

As discussed, CDs have garnered significant interest as highly promising carbon-based fluorescent zero-dimensional nanomaterials [20–22] owing to their unique physicochemical characteristics. These features encompass minimal toxicity, chemical resistance, water solubility, tailored emission, broad absorption spectrum, and photostability [23]. Such characteristics render them suitable for diverse applications, including bioimaging, photocatalysis, light-emitting diodes, and chemical sensors [24]. Numerous studies have documented the use of CDs in sensing systems for detecting proteins, pesticides, metals, and ions like NO2 − [25]. However, CDs often suffer from low quantum yields (QYs), resulting in decreased sensitivity. To overcome these limitations, several studies have suggested that surface modification with elements such as nitrogen, phosphorus, sulfur, and boron can enhance the QYs of CDs [26,27]. By incorporating various heteroatoms in small amounts, one can successfully produce doped CDs. The preparation method in this study can be utilized to develop specialized platforms for accurately identifying different species, such as NO2 −, even complex samples like meat products. Table S1 compares the detection ranges and methodologies used for NO₂− detection using different CDs. The developed safranin-based liquid crystal carbon dots (SLCDs), derived from lemon juice and safranin, demonstrate a broad detection range spanning from 0.1 to 80 μM. Their high efficiency in both fluorescence (FL) and colorimetry techniques enhances flexibility and adaptability in various analytical applications. In contrast, BYDE CDs display a comparable detection range (0.1–100 μM), relying solely on FL [28], which restricts their suitability for colorimetric detection. While both r-CDs [29] and CDs [30] exhibit FL, their detection ranges are narrower, ranging from 5 to 80 μM and 6–75 μM, respectively, reducing their adaptability. Similarly, PA-CDs show a limited sensitivity range (0.01–50 μM) and are detectable only through FL. Additional studies, such as g-CNQDs [31] and CQD-3-Aph [32], utilize both detection methods, though their measurement ranges remain restricted. For instance, g-CNQDs have a detection range of 2–80 μM, whereas CQD-3-Aph covers 0.01–100 μM. SLCDs are particularly noteworthy for their broader detection capabilities and bimodal sensing. This innovation not only improves the sensitivity for detecting trace amounts of NO₂−, but also offers practical advantages in different analytical settings, highlighting the superior performance and flexibility of SLCDs compared to previous devices.

In this study, safranin and lemon extract were used to synthesize SLCDs using a one-step pyrolysis technique. This method is efficient, facile, and time-saving. The SLCDs exhibited strong antioxidant activity, as indicated by 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl DPPH (~ 78 %) and 2,2′-azinobis-(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS) tests (> 80 %). Their cell viability was assessed by using fibroblast (NIH 3 T3) cells to evaluate metabolic activity. SLCDs offer several advantages, such as red emission, easy surface functionalization, excellent biocompatibility, and stability. As a fluorescent probe, these N-doped CDs enable bimodal detection of NO2 −, with NO2 − interacting with the amino groups on the SLCDs surface to form a diazotized substance. This interaction results in significant changes in both colorimetric and fluorescent properties. A cutting-edge optical nanosensor is developed for the rapid and accurate detection of NO2 − in meat products, employing bimodal colorimetric and FL techniques. This sensor exhibits a remarkable ability to achieve colorimetric recognition via dual peaks. Unlike earlier probes limited to colorimetric or FL sensing, this newly developed bimodal chemosensor probe utilizes red emission from CDs and opens up new possibilities for NO2 − for detecting in food specimens.

Safranin was purchased from Millipore Sigma, Germany, while lemons were sourced from a local market. Inorganic salts and amino acids were obtained from Merck, Germany, and all analytical agents were used as received without any additional purification. The water used in this study was purified using the Millipore Milli-Q refinement system (18.25 MΩ cm) in the laboratory.

The synthesis of SLCDs was conducted using a one-step pyrolysis process. Lemons were sliced into small pieces and juiced, and the extracted juice was subsequently centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The resulting liquid was filtered to remove the pulp. Subsequently, 5 mL of lemon extract and 4.8 mg of safranin were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water at room temperature with magnetic stirring for 2 min. Afterward, the mixture was carefully transferred into a 50 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to ambient conditions, the solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was collected after filtration through a 0.22 μm microfilter (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Next, the aqueous dispersion was lyophilized to obtain the purified solid SLCDs in powder form. For subsequent experiments, the SLCDs were solubilized and diluted using Milli-Q water. The entire process is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

Nitrite detection was conducted using the following procedures: Different concentrations of nitrite solutions were prepared, with 100 μL of solution mixed with 500 μL of phosphate-buffered saline solution (0.1 mM, pH 6.0) in a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube.To this mixture, 600 μL of SLCDs (30 mg/mL) was thoroughly mixed for 30 s using a vortex. After a 15 min incubation at 45 °C in a water bath, the specimens were analyzed using UV–vis and FL spectroscopy. The FL measurements were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 510 nm, while UV–vis spectra were obtained at 533 nm and 386 nm.

To evaluate the selectivity of the SLCDs probe for NO2 − detection, various substances, including mono, di, and tri-valent salts, sodium acetate, amino acids, and lactose, were introduced into the sensing system as potential interferences. The tests were repeated three times to ensure the stability and reliability of the results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of nitrite in commercial products
ce:section.ce:para: Meat products were acquired from a local market. The NO2 − extraction from the samples was carried out following the protocol described by Hu et al. [33]. Initially, 5 g of the sample was transferred to a 150 mL volumetric flask, followed by the addition of 80 mL of water and 1 mL of 1 mol/L potassium hydroxide solution. An ultrasonic extraction was used for 30 min, with periodic shaking every 5 min to ensure complete dispersion of the solid phase. Afterward, the sample was placed in a water bath at 75 °C for 5 min, then removed and allowed to cool to ambient temperature. The contents were then transferred into a 100 mL volumetric flask and diluted with water to the mark, flollwed by vigorous shaking. The solution was filtered using a microfilter and then centrifuged to separate the supernatant, which was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for future utilization.

In this study, SLCDs were synthesized using a one-step pyrolysis technique and characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to assess their morphologies, elemental compositions, and chemical structures. The TEM image of SLCDs (Fig. 2a) reveals that the SLCDs are uniformly distributed with a particle size ranging from 1.8 to 6.8 nm. The HRTEM image in the inset of Fig. 2a shows a lattice spacing of ~0.37 nm. The average particle diameter is calculated to be 3.29 nm using the ImageJ software, with the results depicted in Fig. 2b. The optical properties of SLCDs were investigated using UV–vis absorption, FL excitation, and emission spectra. A UV–vis absorption peak is observed at 533 nm, as shown in Fig. 2c. The CDs display a characteristic FL spectrum dependent on the excitation, with a maximum emission peak at 611 nm when excited at 520 nm (Fig. 2d). The FL intensity of the SLCDs remains nearly constant under continuous Xe lamp irradiation for 60 min, as depicted in Figs. S1 and S2. The synthesized SLCDs possess exceptional resistance to photobleaching and pH stability within the neutral range, making them an ideal fluorescent probe for metal ion with high sensitivity and cell imaging cells.

Furthermore, the elemental compositions and chemical structures of SLCDs were analyzed using FTIR and XPS. The FTIR spectrum provides detailed information on the functional groups present, which can be identified through their distinct absorption peaks, as depicted in Fig. 2e. A broad absorption band observed between 3200 and 3400 cm−1 corresponds to OH bond stretching vibrations, indicating the existence of hydroxyl groups likely from water and carboxylic acids in the lemon extract. Moreover, peaks observed at 2920 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 are associated with the stretching of aliphatic CH bonds, confirming the presence of methylene and methyl groups (sp3-hybridized carbon atoms). A distinct peak at 1730 cm−1 indicates the stretching of the CO bond, which is commonly found in compounds like carboxylic acids or esters, likely due to citric acid from lemon juice. The peak around 1600 cm−1 corresponds to the CC stretching vibrations, suggesting the presence of aromatic rings in safranin, an aromatic dye. In addition, the peaks detected at 1450 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1 are associated with CH and OH bond bending, respectively. These peaks indicate the existence of aliphatic groups, –COOH groups, and phenolic moieties. In the spectral range of 1250–1050 cm−1, CO stretching vibrations indicate the presence of ether and alcohol groups, likely formed through esterification of citric acid or other oxygen-containing compounds during synthesis. These peaks confirm that the CDs consist of various functional groups derived from the precursor materials. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. S3) of the SLCDs exhibits a wide peak at 19.1 degree, which corresponds to the (002) hkl plane according to the JCPDS# 26–1076. The interlayer spacing (d) of the CDs (0.387 nm) is greater than that of the graphitic interlayer spacing (0.33 nm), indicating a broader characteristic. This confirms the low crystallinity of SLCDs. Similar findings have been reported elsewhere [34].

Assessing the stability of nanoparticle size is a crucial factor in colloidal systems. This study assesses the stability of SLCDs in a water-based dispersion. The dimensions are determined from the dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiment, as depicted in Figs. S4 and S5. Initial results reveal an average SLCD size of approximately 2.8 nm. Consistent measurements of dimensions are vital for confirming stability. In addition, a DLS study conducted over a week shows minimal alterations in SLCD size, indicating their stability and an extended operating period for these materials.

The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3a) reveals that the SLCDs consist of carbon (52.54 %), nitrogen (0.93 %), and oxygen (46.63 %). The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3b) can be deconvoluted into five peaks at 289.3 eV, 289.0 eV, 287.1 eV, 285.5 eV, and 285.0 eV, corresponding to CO, CO, CN, CC, and CC bonds, respectively [35–37]. Fig. 3c shows the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum, which displays two distinct peaks at 399.8 eV and 401.6 eV, suggesting the presence of CN and NH bonds. The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3d) is deconvoluted into three peaks at 532.1 eV, 533.0 eV, and 533.7 eV, belonging to CO, CO, and C-OH/C–O–C bands, respectively [38]. These findings further support the FTIR results.

To examine the oxidation resistance of SLCDs, DPPH and ABTS assays were performed. The DPPH free radical is a commonly used method for assessing the antioxidant activity of various substances [22]. The assay measures spectrophotometric changes resulting from the reaction between DPPH• and antioxidants, recognized for its efficiency, simplicity, and cost-effectiveness. DPPH is a stable free radical attributed to its N atoms with unpaired electrons and a distinctive molecular structure comprising three benzene rings. The interaction between DPPH and an antioxidant involves the capture of a hydrogen radical, forming a DPPH–H complex, which is signaled by a color shift from purple to pale yellow [39]. The study investigates the efficacy of SLCDs in eliminating DPPH radicals across concentrations from 0 to 550 μg/mL, as depicted in Fig. 4a. Notably, the DPPH scavenging activity increases with rising concentrations of CDs up to 400 μg/mL. Beyond this, antioxidant efficiency shows only minor changes, reaching a saturation point at approximately 550 μg/mL. Furthermore, the ABTS scavenging experiment is conducted following a previously reported procedure [40–42]. The SLCD solution is diluted to different concentrations. The ABTS radical cation (ABTS+) was diluted using ultrapure water until the absorbance at 734 nm reached 0.70 (±0.02) cm−1. Afterward, the ABTS+ solution was mixed with varying concentrations of SLCDs and incubated at room temperature in the dark for 30 min, followed by absorbance measurement at 734 nm. SLCDs show concentration-dependent ABTS• + scavenging activity, as evidenced by a color change from blue-green to colorless (Fig. 4b). A decrease in absorbance was observed with increasing SLCD concentration, confirming the relationship between CD concentration and ABTS• + scavenging efficiency. Notably, the scavenging activity increases significantly with the concentrations of CDs, reaching a maximum of 300 μg/mL before reaching a saturation point. The incorporation of CDs dopants improves radical scavenging by promoting efficient electrons, potentially leading to proton transfer or adduct formation. The hydroxyl groups on CDs enable the effective reduction and quenching of ABTS• and DPPH• free radicals. Fig. 4c illustrates the radical scavenging performance of SLCDs. During antioxidant tests, CDs undergo chemical tranformations, allowing them to effectively eliminate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and counteract the effects of free radicals. Their surface is abundant in functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, which play a vital role in their antioxidant activity. Hydroxyl groups have the ability to transfer hydrogen atoms to free radicals, transforming them into less reactive entities like water or alcohol [43,44]. Carboxyl groups engage in redox reactions by donating or absorbing electrons, thereby stabilizing free radicals and ultimately reducing oxidative stress. Similarly, amino groups neutralize detrimental radicals by supplying electrons or hydrogen atoms. Furthermore, the CDs possess conjugated π-electron systems, which facilitate effective electron delocalization, enhancing their capacity to interact with and neutralize free radicals. Through hydrogen atom transfer and electron transfer mechanisms, CDs can stabilize themselves and mitigate oxidative damage [45,46]. These processes highlight the significance of surface functionalization and the electronic structure of CDs in their antioxidant activity.

To ensure an optimal signal response, several key factors are thoroughly examined, including pH, SLCD concentration, reaction time, and incubation temperature. These parameters are carefully optimized to yield the best detection results. For pH optimization, the pH of the buffer is adjusted across a broad range, from 1.8 to 12.2, with results depicted in Fig. 5a and b. Notably, within the pH range of 1.8–6.1, the absorption ratio and FL quenching rate of SLCDs exhibit a noticeable increase following the reaction with NO2 −. Between pH 6.0 and 12.0, a noticeable decrease in both the absorption ratios and FL quenching rate is observed with increasing pH. It is worth noting that the absorption ratio and FL quenching rate attain their maximum value at pH 6.0, making it the ideal pH for further investigation.

Furthermore, the impact of varying SLCD concentrations on the effectiveness of colorimetric and FL investigation was explored by evaluating changes in the absorbance ratio and FL quenching efficiency of NO2 − across different SLCDs concentration systems. In order to examine these effects on the activity of the analyte, a solution of volume of 1.5 mL was used, with 500 μL of SLCDs. The optimal reaction concentration was determined by observing the color change in both colorimetric and FL probes upon the introduction of NO2 − into the detection system. As depicted in Fig. 5c and d, the FL intensity ratio follows a trend consistent with the absorption intensity ratio. At a concentration of 15 mg/mL, the NO2 − to SLCDs demonstrate the highest FL quenching efficacy and UV–vis absorbance intensity ratio, indicating this is the optimal concentration. The study also examined the effect of different incubation temperatures on the detection system. Significant variations in the probe's detection behavior and UV–vis analysis capability is observed at different reaction temperatures, as observed in Fig. 5e and f. The probe performs best at 45 °C, thus establishing this temperature as ideal for subsequent experiments. Additionally, the study examines the influence of reaction time. The UV–vis absorbance intensity ratio and FL quenching efficacy of SLCDs are depicted in Fig. 6a and b, showcasing their temporal changes following NO2 −. Both the absorbance ratio and FL quenching efficacy increase progressively over time. After 15 min, the absorbance ratio stabilizes, reflecting a consistent rate of FL quenching. Thus, a reaction time of 15 min is identified as ideal for SLCDs and NO2 − interactions.

A study was conducted to examine the UV–vis absorbance and FL intensities of the SLCDs at different NO2 − concentrations based on previously optimized conditions. This investigation aimed to assess the sensitivity of this probe. As depicted in Fig. 7a, the absorbance intensity at 533 nm decreases gradually with increasing concentration of NO2 −, while a new peak appears at 386 nm, with its absorbance intensity rising steadily. This shift in absorption intensity is attributed to the interaction between NO2 − and amino surface functionalities, forming a diazotized substance. A strong linear relationship (Fig. 7b) is observed between the NO2 − concentration and the absorbance intensity ratio (A386/A533), particularly within the range of 0–60 μM (R2 = 0.998). The detection limit for NO2 − is determined to be 0.19 μM based on 3δ/S rule [47]. This indicates that SLCDs can serve as an effective colorimetric probe for NO2 − detection. The FL response of the SLCDs fluorescent probe is further evaluated across varying NO2 − concentrations. Fig. 7c illustrates the decrease in FL intensity at 602 nm as the NO2 − concentration increases from 0 to 80 μM. Fig. 7d demonstrates a strong positive correlation between the FL quenching rate ((F0 F)/F0) and NO2 − concentration within the 0–40 μM range (R2 = 0.991). In addition, the detection limit for NO2 − is calculated to be 0.144 μM. Based on these findings, the fluorescent probe developed in this study is highly effective for NO2 − detection. Table 1 compares the proposed technique with previously reported methods for NO2 − detection, highlighting that the bimodal detection probe offers a superior detection limit and linear range. These results confirm that the suggested technique is both responsive and reliable for NO2 − detection.

The evaluation of the current probe's performance for target detection hinges not only on sensitivity but also on selectivity. To assess the selectivity of the projected chemosensing ability for NO2 −, the absorbance ratio and FL signal are measured in the presence of various electrolytes and amino acids at a concentration of 150 μM. The outcomes, presented in Fig. 8a and b, include various ions such as Na+, K+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cl−, I−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, NO3 −, as well as organic compounds like acetate, Gly, Cys, Lys, Asp, Ser, and lactose. It is evident that only NO2 − has a notable impact on the A386/A533 and (F0 F)/F0 of the detection probe. Supplementary electrolytes and biomolecules, even at a concentrations up to ~8 times higher than NO2 −, do not cause significant changes in UV–vis absorbance ratios or FL quenching. These results showcase the excellent analytical performance of our detection system, making it ideal for the accurate determination of NO2 − levels in actual food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible mechanistic pathway of nitrite sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching mechanism was further investigated to explore the selective detection of NO2 − by SLCDs. Fig. 9a depicts the UV–vis absorption spectra of NO2 − alongside the excitation and emission peaks of SLCDs. The data reveal that the distinct UV–vis peak of NO2 − falls within the 300–400 nm window. It is worth mentioning that this peak does not coincide with the excitation and emission peaks of SLCDs, suggesting that the quenched FL of SLCDs by NO2 − is not driven by the inner filter effect or Forster resonance energy transfer [55]. The changes in UV–vis absorption, FTIR, and FL lifetime before and after the interaction of SLCDs with NO2 − were further examined. The results indicate a prominent absorption peak at 533 nm for SLCDs (Fig. 9b), whereas NO2 − exhibits a peak at 354 nm. Upon exposure of SLCDs to NO2 −, a distinct absorption peak emerges at 386 nm, specifying the formation of diazo groups (−N ≡ N−). The quenching of FL emission in SLCDs through diazotization is ascribed to several factors, including the formation of non-fluorescent diazonium salts, interruption of π-conjugation, and electron transport mechanisms. In addition, the transfer of energy to diazonium groups, the production of non-fluorescent complexes, and alterations in surface passivation contribute to the reduction of emissions. These processes collectively lead to a substantial reduction in the FL of the CDs during the diazotization reaction [56–58]. At the same time, the original absorption peak of SLCDs at 533 nm reduced and shifted towards a longer wavelength, resulting in noticeable changes in color. In addition, zeta-potentials (ζ) and (DLS) analysis were conducted, with the ζ-potentials of SLCDs and NO2 − measured as 7.46 and − 3.61 mV, respectively. Interestingly, the combination of SLCDs and NO2 − results in a potential of 5.82 mV. It is evident that the average particle size of SLCDs increases from 2.89 nm to 4.06 nm following their interaction with NO2 −. This suggests that the interaction between NO2 − and the surface groups of SLCDs has led to the formation of new substances [59]. A new peak at 1633 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of SLCDs (Fig. 9c) indicates the presence of the -N=N- bond [60], likely due to the interaction between NO2 − and the amino group of SLCDs, resulting in the construction of a diazotized substance. Measurements are taken both before and after the addition of NO2 −. The decay curves in Fig. 9d show that the FL lifetimes of SLCDs remain consistently stable, ranging from 2.21 to 2.29 ns, even with varying concentrations of NO2 −. Analysis using the Stern-Volmer equation reveals that the quenching caused by NO2 − is predominantly attributed to static quenching. Although, the UV–vis spectra of SLCDs experience a change, the FL lifetime remains largely unaffected, further supporting static quenching as the dominant mechanism.

To assess the detection of NO2 − in real samples, meat products were tested using a colorimetric and FL-based chemosensor probe. The nitrite content in commercial meat is calculated to be 5.8 mg/Kg. A standard solution of NO2 − was then introduced to the specimen and subsequently identified. The probe's measurements closely match the expected high concentrations, with recovery rates ranging from 89.9 % to 104.2 %. The results clearly show that the proposed probe is highly accurate and reliable for the rapid detection of NO2 − in meat products. Furthermore, NO2 − contents are quantified in cured chicken, jerked beef, salted fish, and a milky drink using fluorimetry and colorimetry techniques. In cured chicken, the concentration of NO2 − detected using FL is noted to be 4.08 mg/kg, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.69 % while the UV–vis detection method measures 3.96 mg/kg, with a RSD of 3.03 %. The consistent results obtained from both methods indicate the reliability of the detection process.

An evaluation of the non-cytotoxic behavior and biocompatibility of SLCDs was performed using an MTT assay on fibroblast (NIH 3 T3) cells. In particular, the cells were cultured and incubated with different concentrations of SLCDs at 37 °C for 24 h, followed by a viability assessment. As shown in Fig. 10 , over 90 % of fibroblast cells remain viable at a concentration of 400 μg/mL. Based on these findings, it can be concluded that SLCDs demonstrate low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility.

This study presented a straightforward technique for synthesizing N-doped SLCDs, which hold promise for applications as antioxidants and nitrite sensors in the food industry. The radical scavenging activity of these SLCDs was assessed using the DPPH and ABTS assays. The SLCDs exhibited excellent antioxidant activity, with DPPH and ABTS assays showing scavenging efficiencies of approximately 78 % and > 80 %, respectively. Furthermore, toxicity was assessed using the MTT assay, revealing minimal toxicity to human cell lines. These outcomes highlighted the SLCDs as both effective antioxidants and valuable tools for biomedical research. A novel probe was developed for the bimodal detection of NO2 −, offering a simple, affordable, and time-efficient approach. Upon the addition of NO2 − to the SLCDs solution, a reaction occurred between NO2 − and the amino functionalities on the SLCDs surface, resulting in the formation of a diazotized substance. This interaction led to noticeable changes in the solution: a permanent shift in color from vibrant red to a lighter red hue and a gradual decrease in red FL intensity. Over the concentration range of 0–60 μM, both the absorption intensity ratio and FL quenching rate exhibited a linear correlation with NO2 − concentration. Similarly, NO2 − concentration displayed a linear relationship with the FL quenching rate in the range of 0–40 μM. Detection limits for NO2 − were found to be 0.19 μM and 0.144 μM using colorimetric and fluorometric methods, respectively. This approach has proven effective for testing meat products and provides substantial advantages for the precise and reliable detection of NO2 − in food samples.

Mohammed S. Alkaltham: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Suleiman A. Althawab: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Formal analysis. Tawfiq Alsulami: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Conceptualization. Abdulhakeem Alzahrani: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to extend their sincere appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, for funding this work through the project number (RSPD2024R917).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111624.
